This system was designed to improve both military response and succession — ensuring that experienced men were always ready to assume the purple. He took the East himself, with Maximian ruling the West, Constantius Chlorus guarding Gaul and Britain, and Galerius ruling the Balkans.

Diocletian also undertook sweeping administrative, military, and economic reforms. He doubled or tripled the size of the army, divided provinces into smaller units to reduce the power of individual governors, reorganized taxation on a more systematic basis, and issued the Edict on Maximum Prices (301 AD) — an ambitious but ultimately unsuccessful attempt to control inflation. His persecution of Christians (303–311 AD) was the most severe in Roman history. On May 1, 305 AD, he took the unprecedented step of voluntarily abdicating — the only Roman emperor to do so — and retired to his enormous palace at Split (Spalatum) in modern Croatia, where he reportedly grew cabbages.
rulers.coinage: Diocletian's monetary reform of 293–301 AD was the most comprehensive restructuring of Roman currency since Augustus. He introduced a new denomination system: the large silvered bronze follis (about 10g), the argenteus (a pure silver coin reviving the old denarius standard), and reformed gold aurei. The system was marked by mint marks and workshop letters — a systematic approach to quality control that allowed problematic batches to be traced. His portrait type on the follis is among the most austere and imperial in the entire Roman series: a severe, clean-shaven face with a Jupiter-like dignity that embodies the Tetrarchic ideology of the emperor as divine ruler.

rulers.name: Maximian
rulers.overview: Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus was Diocletian's co-Augustus for the West — a tough Pannonian soldier who was persuaded to take the throne by Diocletian and who struggled to enforce the same abdication his colleague did voluntarily.
rulers.biography:
Born around 250 AD in Pannonia (modern Serbia/Hungary), Maximian was an old comrade of Diocletian who was appointed Caesar in 285 AD and then co-Augustus in 286 AD. As the western emperor, he suppressed the revolt of the Bagaudae in Gaul, conducted campaigns in Germany, and coordinated with Constantius Chlorus on the recovery of Britain from the usurper Carausius.

When Diocletian abdicated on May 1, 305 AD, Maximian reluctantly did the same, retiring to southern Italy. But the retirement was brief: when his son Maxentius seized power in Rome in 306 AD, Maximian resumed the purple and became enmeshed in the complex civil wars of the post-Tetrarchic period. He attempted to betray and then depose his son-in-law Constantine, was captured, and committed suicide (or was executed) in 310 AD at Constantine's orders. His end illustrated the difficulty of the Tetrarchic succession theory in practice.
rulers.coinage: Maximian's coinage follows the Tetrarchic template established by Diocletian — the same austere portrait types, the same systematic mint marks, the same denomination structure. His Herculian identity (he was 'Herculius' to Diocletian's 'Iovius') is reflected in some coin types featuring the Herculian club. His folles, argentei, and aurei follow the reformed Diocletianic standards. His western mints at Lugdunum, Milan, and Aquileia produced large quantities of well-struck tetrarchic coinage.

rulers.name: Constantine I
rulers.overview: Flavius Valerius Constantinus — Constantine the Great — was the emperor who converted Rome to Christianity, founded Constantinople, introduced the gold solidus that would anchor the Byzantine economy for a millennium, and permanently transformed the Western world.
rulers.biography:
Born around 272 AD at Naissus (modern Niš, Serbia), Constantine was the son of the western Caesar Constantius Chlorus and his companion Helena (later Saint Helena). When Constantius died at York in 306 AD, the troops acclaimed Constantine as emperor — ignoring the Tetrarchic system completely. He was one of six claimants to the purple at various points between 306 and 313 AD.

His victory over Maxentius at the Milvian Bridge on October 28, 312 AD — traditionally attributed to the Christian Chi-Rho symbol shown to him in a vision — was the turning point. Constantine's Edict of Milan in 313 AD extended tolerance to all religions, including Christianity. He increasingly favored the Christian church with patronage, endowments, and legal privileges. The Council of Nicaea in 325 AD, which he convened and presided over, established Christian orthodoxy and the Nicene Creed.

His founding of Constantinople (dedicated May 11, 330 AD) on the site of the old Greek city of Byzantium created a new eastern capital that would outlast Rome by nearly a thousand years. He reunited the empire under his sole rule in 324 AD by defeating his last rival Licinius. He died on May 22, 337 AD, having been baptized on his deathbed — the last Roman emperor to complete the transition from pagan tradition to Christian faith.
rulers.coinage:
Constantine's coinage records one of the most dramatic religious transformations in history. His early coins carry SOL INVICTVS ('Unconquered Sun') — the pagan solar deity promoted by Aurelian — as the dominant reverse type, showing a gradual transition from solar paganism to Christianity. From around 315 AD the Chi-Rho (☧) begins appearing, increasingly dominant through the 320s and 330s. His famous SPES PVBLIC ('Hope of the State') type, showing the labarum (Christian military standard) spearing a serpent, is one of the most historically charged images in numismatics.

His greatest monetary innovation was the solidus — introduced around 309 AD at 72 to the Roman pound of gold, a slightly lighter standard than the aureus. The solidus maintained its purity essentially unchanged through the entire Byzantine period until the 11th century — over 700 years. He also struck large bronze folles and smaller reduced bronze denominations as the Tetrarchic currency system evolved. His coins are among the most commonly encountered of all late Roman issues.

rulers.name: Constantius II
rulers.overview: Flavius Julius Constantius — Constantius II — was the middle son of Constantine I, the longest-reigning of Constantine's three sons, and the emperor who definitively established Christianity as Rome's state religion while promoting the Arian heresy that split the church.
rulers.biography:
Born on August 7, 317 AD, Constantius became co-emperor after his father's death in 337 AD alongside his brothers Constantine II and Constans, after a massacre of most of the rest of the family engineered by the troops on Constantius's behalf. By 353 AD he had eliminated or outlasted both brothers and ruled the entire empire alone.

Constantius was a serious, hardworking emperor of limited vision who prosecuted theological controversies with all the energy a more gifted ruler might have devoted to military campaigns. He was an Arian Christian — believing Christ was created by, and subordinate to, the Father — and used his imperial authority to impose Arianism on the church, exiling orthodox bishops including Athanasius multiple times. His defeat of the usurper Magnentius (353 AD), his Persian campaigns, and his generally competent administration are overshadowed in the sources by his theological politics.

He died of fever on November 3, 361 AD, and was succeeded by his cousin Julian 'the Apostate,' who reversed his religious policies entirely.
rulers.coinage: Constantius II's most distinctive and numerous coin type is the FEL TEMP REPARATIO ('Happy Times Are Restored') series — huge quantities of small bronze coins, struck at mints across the empire, showing various images: a soldier spearing a fallen horseman, a soldier leading barbarians from a hut, and a phoenix on a globe. These types are among the most common late Roman bronze coins encountered today. His silver siliquae are important for the Chi-Rho christogram and VOT types.

rulers.name: Magnentius
rulers.overview: Flavius Magnus Magnentius was a usurper of Germanic origin who seized the western empire from Constans in 350 AD and struck the most explicitly Christian coinage yet seen in the Roman world before his defeat by Constantius II.
rulers.biography:
Born around 303 AD of Frankish or British origin, Magnentius had risen through the western army to a senior command when he was proclaimed emperor at Autun in January 350 AD. Constans, the western emperor, was killed while fleeing. Magnentius controlled the entire western empire for three years, governing with relative competence and tolerance toward pagans.

His defeat by Constantius II at the Battle of Mursa Major in 351 AD — one of the bloodiest battles in Roman history, reportedly costing 54,000 lives between both sides — decided the civil war. Magnentius retreated to Gaul and Britain, losing more ground until he committed suicide at Lugdunum in August 353 AD. His brother Decentius, commanding in Gaul, killed himself two days later.
rulers.coinage: Magnentius's coinage is among the most interesting of the fourth century. His largest bronzes (AE1 and AE2) bear the most prominent Christian symbols yet placed on Roman coinage — a huge Chi-Rho flanked by Alpha and Omega on the reverse, with SALVS DD NN AVG ET CAES. This dramatic Christian declaration on a usurper's coins suggests he was bidding for the support of the growing Christian population against the Arian Constantius. His portrait types show a strong, somewhat heavy-featured face with the military bearing appropriate to his origin.

rulers.name: Julian 'the Apostate'
rulers.overview: Flavius Claudius Julianus — Julian the Apostate — was Constantine's nephew, a brilliant and bookish philosopher-emperor who attempted to reverse the Christianization of the empire and restore traditional paganism, dying in battle against Persia before he could succeed.
rulers.biography:
Born in 331 or 332 AD in Constantinople, Julian survived the massacre of Constantine's family in 337 AD because of his age — he was perhaps six years old. He was educated in Christian orthodoxy but secretly converted to Neoplatonic paganism as a young man. Constantius II, suspicious of him, kept him in scholarly seclusion until the Gallic frontier emergency of 355 AD forced him to appoint Julian as Caesar in the West. Julian proved an unexpectedly brilliant general, winning major victories against the Alemanni.

When Constantius ordered Julian's crack troops transferred to the Persian front in 360 AD, the troops revolted and proclaimed Julian Augustus. Before the civil war began, Constantius died, and Julian became sole emperor. He immediately reversed the Christianization of the empire: he ended subsidies to the church, reopened pagan temples, required rhetoricians who were Christian to convert to paganism or stop teaching classical literature, and attempted to revive sacrificial religion.

In 363 AD he launched an invasion of Persia — the last major Roman offensive into Mesopotamia. The campaign penetrated to Ctesiphon but could not take the city, and the retreat was disastrous. Julian was mortally wounded by a spear on June 26, 363 AD, reportedly as he plunged into battle without armor. Whether the weapon was Persian or thrown by a Christian soldier in his own army has been debated ever since.
rulers.coinage: Julian's coinage is distinctive and historically fascinating. His large bronze AE1 and AE2 issues — the largest bronze denominations since the early tetrarchy — show a bearded emperor wearing the philosopher's beard he cultivated as a statement of Hellenic paganism (earlier Christian emperors had been clean-shaven or lightly bearded). His reverses explicitly reference pagan religion: a bull sacrifice (SECVRITAS REIPVB), the Gallic double-faced Janus, and the bull with two stars. The bull sacrifice type is his most sought-after coin, directly referencing his revival of animal sacrifice. His gold solidi maintained the Constantinian standard.

groups.name: Late Empire
groups.overview: The Late Empire struggled under the increasing pressure of Germanic migrations and internal division. The empire split definitively in 395 AD into Western and Eastern halves. The West collapsed under barbarian pressure in 476 AD; the East — the Byzantine Empire — endured for another thousand years.

rulers.name: Valentinian I
rulers.overview: Flavius Valentinianus — Valentinian I — was a tough Pannonian soldier who divided the empire with his brother Valens, defended the Rhine and Danube frontiers with energy, and died of an apoplectic fit while furiously haranguing a barbarian delegation.
rulers.biography:
Born July 3, 321 AD, in Cibalae in Pannonia (modern Croatia), Valentinian rose through the military ranks to be chosen as emperor by the army council after Julian's death in 363 AD and Jovian's brief reign. He immediately appointed his brother Valens as co-emperor for the East and devoted his own attention to the critical western frontiers.

Valentinian's reign was characterized by effective military defense. He strengthened the Rhine and upper Danube frontiers with new fortifications, personally commanding major campaigns against the Alemanni and Sarmatians. He was intolerant of corruption and treated the senatorial class harshly. He appointed the general Theodosius (father of the future emperor) to recover Britain from barbarian raids in 367–368 AD.

He died on November 17, 375 AD, at Brigetio on the Danube, from a burst blood vessel during a furious rage at Quadian envoys who appeared before him with insufficient deference. He was fifty-four years old. His sons Gratian (in the West) and Valentinian II (a child) succeeded him.
rulers.coinage: Valentinian I's coinage follows the patterns established in the Constantinian period: gold solidi and smaller fractions (semissis, tremissis), silver siliquae, and abundant small bronze coins. His portrait, with a strong military bearing and the heavy brow of his Pannonian features, is straightforward and unidealized. The RESTITVTOR REIP ('Restorer of the Republic') and GLORIA ROMANORVM ('Glory of the Romans') types are standard for his reign. His western mint production was concentrated at Lugdunum, Milan, and Aquileia.

rulers.name: Valens
rulers.overview: Flavius Valens was Valentinian I's younger brother and eastern co-emperor, whose catastrophic defeat and death at the Battle of Adrianople in 378 AD — where Gothic cavalry destroyed a Roman army and killed the emperor — marked a turning point in Roman military history.
rulers.biography:
Born in 328 AD, Valens had no significant military or administrative experience when his brother made him eastern emperor in 364 AD. He proved a competent if uninspired ruler of the East, generally following his brother's example. His Arian Christianity put him in persistent conflict with the orthodox majority, and he exiled bishops with Constantinian frequency.

The defining event of his reign was the Gothic crisis. In 376 AD, with the Huns pressing from the east, the Visigoths requested permission to cross the Danube and settle within the empire. Valens agreed, seeing potential military recruits. But Roman officials exploited the desperate refugees, and the Goths revolted. Valens hurried back from his Persian negotiations to deal with the crisis.

On August 9, 378 AD, at the Battle of Adrianople in Thrace, Valens engaged the Gothic army before Gratian's reinforcements arrived. The Roman cavalry was driven off, and the infantry — trapped in a killing circle by Gothic cavalry — was massacred. Valens was killed in the rout, possibly burned to death in a farmhouse where he had taken refuge. Perhaps 20,000 Roman soldiers died. The battle demonstrated that barbarian cavalry could defeat Roman infantry in open battle — a fundamental shift in military balance.
rulers.coinage: Valens's coinage parallels that of his brother Valentinian I, using the same types and following the same mint structure in the eastern provinces. His eastern mints — Constantinople, Nicomedia, Cyzicus, Antioch — produced solidi, siliquae, and bronze AE3s and AE4s. Some of his later coins show the anxiety of the Gothic crisis: GLORIA ROMANORVM types depicting the emperor dragging a captive barbarian became increasingly ironic as his actual military situation deteriorated.

rulers.name: Theodosius I
rulers.overview: Flavius Theodosius — Theodosius the Great — was the last emperor to rule a unified Roman Empire. He settled the Gothic problem through negotiation, made Christianity the official state religion, suppressed paganism, and divided the empire between his two sons on his death.
rulers.biography:
Born on January 11, 347 AD, in Hispania, Theodosius was the son of Count Theodosius, Valentinian I's successful general. After his father's execution (on apparently political grounds) in 375 AD, he retired to Spain. Following Adrianople's disaster, Gratian recalled him and made him eastern emperor on January 19, 379 AD.

Theodosius settled the Gothic problem by the Treaty of 382 AD, allowing the Visigoths to settle within the empire as autonomous foederati (allied troops) — a pragmatic solution that avoided another Adrianople but fundamentally changed the empire's ethnic and military composition. In 380 AD he issued the Edict of Thessalonica, making Nicene Christianity the official state religion of the empire and beginning the systematic repression of paganism that culminated in the closure of pagan temples in 391–392 AD.

He defeated two western usurpers — Magnus Maximus in 388 AD and Eugenius in 394 AD — briefly reuniting the empire under his sole rule. He died on January 17, 395 AD, at Milan, aged forty-seven, leaving the empire divided permanently between his two sons: Arcadius (East) and Honorius (West).
rulers.coinage: Theodosius's coinage is extensive and well-documented, covering both his eastern and (after 394) unified western mints. His portrait types are handsome and classicizing — a contrast to some of the rougher images of his predecessors. His VICTORIA AVGGG types (three Gs for three Augusti — himself and his two sons) celebrate military victories; his CONCORDIA AVGGG types emphasize dynastic harmony. His Missoria — large silver presentation dishes like the famous Missorium of 388 AD in Madrid — give a more detailed portrait than his coins alone.

rulers.name: Honorius
rulers.overview: Flavius Honorius was the western emperor during the catastrophic sack of Rome by the Visigoths in 410 AD — an event that shocked the Mediterranean world — yet he survived, reportedly more concerned about his pet chicken than the fall of the eternal city.
rulers.biography:
Born on September 9, 384 AD, Honorius was the younger son of Theodosius I and received the western empire on his father's death in 395 AD at age ten. His long minority was dominated by his general Stilicho, a man of Vandal origin who was the real power in the West. Under Stilicho's management, Alaric's Visigoths were repeatedly bought off or militarily checked.

After Honorius had Stilicho executed in 408 AD on suspicion of treason, the western empire's military capacity collapsed. Alaric sacked Rome in August 410 AD — the first time the city had fallen to an enemy in 800 years. The shock throughout the Mediterranean world was enormous; Saint Augustine began writing the City of God partly in response. Honorius survived by staying safely in Ravenna, which had replaced Milan as the western capital.

Honorius continued to reign, increasingly passively, until his death on August 15, 423 AD, aged thirty-eight. He left no heir, and a succession crisis followed. The story that he thought the messenger announcing Rome's fall was reporting the death of his favorite hen Roma (named for the city) may be apocryphal, but it captures something real about his priorities.
rulers.coinage: Honorius's gold solidi and silver siliquae followed Theodosian patterns but show a gradual coarsening of portrait quality as western mint organization declined. His most distinctive types show him in military dress holding a standard with the Chi-Rho and the legend GLORIA ROMANORVM — an increasingly ironic claim as barbarians dismembered the western provinces. His Milan and Ravenna mints produced the bulk of his coinage; some types also struck at Rome and other western cities.

rulers.name: Arcadius
rulers.overview: Flavius Arcadius was the eastern emperor and elder son of Theodosius I — a weak, dominated figure whose court intrigues and minister succession provided much dramatic material for historians while the real work of governing the East was done by others.
rulers.biography:
Born around 377 AD, Arcadius received the eastern empire when he was about eighteen. His reign was dominated by powerful ministers and court factions, above all by the praetorian prefect Eutropius (a eunuch who became the first of his kind to hold consular rank) and then by his formidable wife Eudoxia. The conflict between Eudoxia and John Chrysostom, Archbishop of Constantinople, whom she had exiled twice, is one of the major ecclesiastical dramas of the period.

Under Arcadius's nominal rule, the East weathered the Visigothic crisis with greater success than the West — partly through skillful diplomacy that redirected Alaric westward. He died on May 1, 408 AD, aged around thirty, leaving his seven-year-old son Theodosius II as emperor. Despite the weakness of his personal rule, the eastern empire's structural stability meant it would endure far longer than the West.
rulers.coinage: Arcadius's coinage follows the standard late Roman patterns. His portrait types show a young man in the imperial diadem, increasingly stylized toward the flat, frontal, hieratic style that would characterize Byzantine coinage. His gold solidi from the Constantinople, Nicomedia, and other eastern mints are common in the numismatic record. His small bronze AE3 and AE4 pieces with GLORIA ROMANORVM and similar reverses are frequently encountered.

rulers.name: Anthemius
rulers.overview: Procopius Anthemius was one of the last western emperors — a Greek from Constantinople imposed on the West by the eastern emperor Leo I, who tried and failed to reverse the collapse of Roman power in the West.
rulers.biography:
Born around 420 AD, Anthemius came from an aristocratic Byzantine family and served as a general under the emperor Marcian. The eastern emperor Leo I chose him as western emperor in 467 AD, hoping to use him as an instrument for a joint east-west reconquest of Africa from the Vandals. The great expedition of 468 AD — the largest Roman fleet assembled in generations — was catastrophically defeated by the Vandal king Gaiseric using fire ships in the Bay of Tunis.

Without the promised eastern support and facing the increasingly powerful barbarian general Ricimer (who was, ironically, his son-in-law), Anthemius's position deteriorated. In 472 AD Ricimer besieged Rome itself. The city was sacked in July 472 AD, and Anthemius was captured and beheaded. He was the second-to-last western emperor of any significance; the West limped on for four more years until Odoacer deposed Romulus Augustulus in 476 AD.
rulers.coinage: Anthemius struck the standard late western imperial denominations — gold solidi from the Ravenna and Rome mints, and some silver siliquae. His portrait types are notable for attempting to echo the stronger imagery of earlier imperial coinage, including types with a helmeted imperial bust that reach back to Constantinian and even earlier traditions. His coinage is relatively scarce, reflecting his short reign and the limited resources of the collapsing western administration.
